primary_diagnosis.name: Pulmonary hypertension with decompensating right ventricular failure (suspected)

pathophysiology_summary: Pulmonary hypertension is a sustained elevation of pulmonary arterial pressure from disease of the pulmonary vasculature, lung disease, left-heart disease, or chronic thromboembolism. The right ventricle, a thin-walled chamber built for a low-pressure circuit, must generate progressively higher pressures; it hypertrophies (a palpable parasternal right ventricular heave), the pulmonary valve closes against high pressure (a loud pulmonary component of the second heart sound, loud P2), and eventually the RV dilates and fails. Right ventricular failure raises systemic venous pressure (a markedly elevated JVP with a prominent 'a' wave from forceful atrial contraction against a stiff RV), producing tender hepatic congestion, abdominal fullness/ascites and peripheral oedema; a right-sided S3 reflects RV volume overload. Symptoms are progressive exertional dyspnoea and fatigue, with resting hypoxaemia as disease advances. The pivotal high-risk feature here is EXERTIONAL syncope/presyncope: with a fixed high pulmonary vascular resistance, the right ventricle cannot increase cardiac output during exertion, so systemic (and cerebral) perfusion falls - exertional syncope in pulmonary hypertension is a marker of severe disease and a high risk of sudden cardiac death, mandating strict activity restriction. The predominance of right-sided signs with the ABSENCE of orthopnoea/PND and bibasal crackles distinguishes this from left-sided heart failure; the chronic progressive course (rather than sudden pleuritic pain) distinguishes it from acute pulmonary embolism; and a loud P2 (rather than a basal ejection murmur) distinguishes it from aortic stenosis. Diagnosis requires echocardiography (to estimate pulmonary pressures and assess RV function), and ultimately right heart catheterisation, plus a work-up for the cause; treatment depends on the group (pulmonary vasodilators for pulmonary arterial hypertension, treating the underlying lung/left-heart disease, anticoagulation/endarterectomy for chronic thromboembolic disease). None of this can be delivered over telehealth, so the correct action is urgent assessment with strict rest.

differential.diagnosis: Pulmonary hypertension with right ventricular failure
differential.evidence_basis: Progressive exertional dyspnoea with exertional presyncope, ankle swelling and abdominal fullness suggests right-heart strain from pulmonary hypertension.
Also causes exertional dyspnoea and oedema, but the absence of orthopnoea/PND/bibasal crackles with predominantly right-sided signs points away from a primarily left-sided cause.

Breathlessness with hypoxaemia warrants considering PE, but the chronic progressive course over months rather than sudden pleuritic onset makes acute PE less likely (chronic thromboembolic PH remains a possible cause).
Can cause exertional dyspnoea/syncope, but a loud P2 (not a basal ejection murmur to the carotids) points to the pulmonary rather than the systemic circuit.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise likely pulmonary hypertension/right heart failure, weight the exertional syncope heavily, mandate rest, and arrange urgent echocardiography/assessment.

differential.diagnosis: Pulmonary hypertension with decompensating right ventricular failure
differential.evidence_basis: Exertional presyncope (DI-001) + progressive exertional dyspnoea with resting hypoxaemia (DI-002) + systemic venous congestion (DI-003), with (sealed) an RV heave, loud P2, right S3 and markedly raised JVP, and no left-sided features (DI-004), indicates pulmonary hypertension with right heart failure.
No orthopnoea/PND/bibasal crackles; the congestion is right-sided.
Kept in mind (chronic thromboembolic disease can cause PH), but the chronic course argues against acute PE.
The loud P2 and right-sided signs point to the pulmonary circuit, not aortic stenosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is pulmonary hypertension with right heart failure and a high-risk exertional-syncope marker needing urgent echocardiography/specialist assessment and strict rest.

differential.diagnosis: Pulmonary hypertension with decompensating right ventricular failure - presumptive, pending echocardiography and right heart catheterisation
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation requires echocardiography (pulmonary pressures/RV function) and ultimately right heart catheterisation; the correct action is urgent assessment with strict activity restriction.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is high-risk pulmonary hypertension/right heart failure needing urgent same-day assessment and strict rest - not a routine referral or reassurance about deconditioning.

red_flags.description: Exertional presyncope/near-syncope (high-risk marker of severe pulmonary hypertension)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exertional syncope in pulmonary hypertension indicates the right ventricle cannot augment output on exertion - a marker of severe disease and high sudden-death risk mandating strict activity restriction and urgent assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued exertion risks a syncopal collapse or sudden cardiac death.

red_flags.description: Progressive exertional dyspnoea with resting hypoxaemia (SpO2 92%)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Resting hypoxaemia with a short, progressive course indicates significant disease and limited reserve.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognition delays urgent assessment of advancing right-heart failure.

red_flags.description: Systemic venous congestion (ankle oedema, abdominal fullness) from right ventricular failure
red_flags.clinical_significance: Peripheral oedema and hepatic congestion/ascites indicate decompensating right-heart failure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing congestion and decompensation progress without treatment.

red_flags.description: Right ventricular heave, loud P2, right-sided S3, and markedly elevated JVP (7 cm) with a prominent 'a' wave, and tender hepatomegaly on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs are the cardinal features of pulmonary hypertension with right ventricular failure; auscultation, JVP and abdominal examination cannot be done over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination; the correct action is urgent in-person assessment.

red_flags.description: Elevated pulmonary artery pressure and RV dysfunction on echocardiography/right heart catheterisation - pending
red_flags.clinical_significance: Echocardiography estimates pulmonary pressures and RV function, and right heart catheterisation confirms and characterises pulmonary hypertension; these cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires specialist investigation; the correct action is urgent referral/assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Progressive exertional dyspnoea with new exertional syncope and oedema is not simple deconditioning and warrants urgent assessment.
Exertional syncope in pulmonary hypertension is a high-risk marker of sudden death; failing to weight it, or allowing continued exertion, is dangerous.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Mislabelling as left-sided heart failure or asthma/COPD
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The predominance of right-sided signs with no orthopnoea/PND/crackles points to pulmonary hypertension/right heart failure, which needs echocardiography rather than empirical diuretics/bronchodilators.
High-risk pulmonary hypertension needs urgent echocardiography/specialist assessment and strict activity restriction, not a routine referral.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of a right ventricular heave and auscultation of a loud P2/right-sided S3
Assessment of JVP height and waveform (prominent 'a' wave)
Accurate clinician-measured oxygenation, respiratory rate and blood pressure
Echocardiography (pulmonary pressures/RV function) and right heart catheterisation
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day/next-day in-person assessment with urgent echocardiography and specialist (respiratory/cardiology) evaluation for pulmonary hypertension; mandate strict activity restriction (pace well below breathlessness/dizziness, avoid exertion) given the exertional-syncope sudden-death risk, and call an ambulance (000) for chest pain, severe breathlessness at rest, or an actual faint.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient it is deconditioning, allowing continued exertion, or arranging only a routine referral, risks an exertional syncopal collapse or sudden cardiac death and delays treatment of decompensating right-heart failure.
